content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Daniil Komov on UnsplashMy name is Ryan Garfinkel, I just finished my undergraduate degree in computer science (May 2026), and I’m about to begin my PhD focusing on AI and software systems. I spent the last four years primarily building full stack web apps where AI tools started taking a bigger role in the development process. During my first year, AI tools like ChatGPT and copilot weren’t widely used. But by the end, they had become a near-requirement in industry.
However, AI tools can make very simple, and sometimes costly, mistakes. I remember asking ChatGPT once how many r’s are in the word “strawberry” and it got the wrong answer. I’ve also seen posts on LinkedIn about how it accidentally deleted an entire database. Even small things, like matching my preferred code style, it doesn’t always get right.
Now that I’m switching gears towards my PhD, my undergraduate work may not seem relevant at first. Going from web development to AI and systems research seems like a massive jump. While the translation isn’t direct, the processes and practice of the software development life cycle, writing code that made it from localhost to production, greatly benefited me. It strengthened my attention to detail and gave me experience working collaboratively with other developers using project management tools like JIRA and GitHub.
As a researcher (or anyone in the field really), staying up to date is a requirement. Our practices are rapidly changing with the adoption of AI, where it only takes minutes to bring ideas to life. This has made hackathons easier, building large full stack apps in hours, and building prototypes for research to test our hypotheses. While optimistic about the potential of AI, I’m hesitant when it comes to fully adopting their use. At the moment, they’re prone to hallucinations, making them less reliable for major tasks. Part of the work I’m interested in pursuing is finding ways to bridge AI with formal reasoning methods to improve reliability, security, and trustworthiness.
Communication is also a big part of being a researcher. One of the most important parts of the scientific method comes at the end, sharing your work, which often comes in the form of writing a research paper. I haven’t always considered myself to be a good writer, but over the years I’ve had opportunities to practice and improve through class assignments. Writing, like coding, gets better the more you practice, which is one of my motivations for being here on Medium.
Throughout my journey as a PhD student, I want to share the lessons I’ve learned and cover information on topics I find interesting. As I mentioned earlier, I’m focusing my PhD on AI and systems, so I think I’ll be focusing on that for a while. My advice to those in the field or thinking about joining the field is to be an active participant in your learning. I learned much more outside of my classes about deploying software and using libraries which made it possible for me to make more technically complex apps. Take advantage of opportunities like hackathons because the stories you can tell afterward about what you did to build that project are valuable, regardless whether you won or not.
